Truck accident cases are fundamentally different from standard car accident claims. FMCSA regulations, black box data, driver logs, and carrier liability all come into play — and critical evidence can disappear within days if not preserved immediately. Georgia's 2-year statute of limitations means time is already working against you.

reddit_title: Got hit by a semi-truck in Columbus, GA — what should I do to protect my legal claim?
reddit_body:
First, make sure you've called 911 and sought medical attention even if you feel okay — adrenaline masks injuries and a medical record creates an important paper trail. If it's safe to do so, photograph everything at the scene: the truck's DOT number, license plate, damage, road conditions, and any witnesses. Do not give a recorded statement to the trucking company's insurance without speaking to an attorney first.

Truck accident cases in Georgia involve layers that regular car accidents don't — FMCSA compliance, driver logs, black box (ECM) data, and potential liability across the driver, carrier, and possibly the cargo loader. That evidence has a short shelf life. An attorney can send a spoliation letter quickly to force the trucking company to preserve it. Georgia's statute of limitations is 2 years, but waiting hurts your case.

If you're not sure where to start finding the right attorney, a free matching service like wreckmatch.com can connect you with lawyers who specifically handle commercial truck accident cases in Georgia — no upfront cost and no obligation. Good luck, and feel free to ask follow-up questions here.
